title: Get rid of lip filler bumps. How to avoid lip filler lumps.
filename: Get rid of lip filler bumps. How to avoid lip filler lumps..en.vtt
excerpt: Lift filler lumps and bumps are they normal what to do about them and how do you avoid them in the first place on the show I'm going to discuss the important things you need to know if you're a patient or a clinician about this particular topic firstly lumps after a procedure are…

segments.text: lift filler lumps and bumps are they normal what to do about them and how do you avoid them in the first place on the show I'm going to discuss the important things you need to know if you're a patient or a clinician about this particular topic firstly lumps after a procedure are actually relatively common because lumps can be caused by the procedure itself simply putting a needle into the tissue can cause a small bruise or hematoma that causes a lump that type of lump tends to get better on its own and it tends to be tender when you touch it so that's the way you can differentiate it the other kind of lumps we want to be aware of are lumps that are caused by the filler itself so that's just a lump caused by injection technique so a lump of filler or a Bolis of filler that the patient can either see or feel that bothers them is also an easy type of lump to solve because if you think about it it's just a lump of filler you should be able to massage it until it softens and Fades into the tissue so this is a non-inflammatory lump non-inflammatory lesion as we call them it's a massage lump or it may go with time so many procedures is there's
you avoid them in the first place on the

things you need to know if you're a
patient or a clinician about this
particular topic firstly lumps after a
procedure are actually relatively common
because lumps can be caused by the

lump non-inflammatory lesion as we call
them it's a massage lump or it may go
with time so many procedures is there's

a combination of swelling plus newly placed filler that hasn't integrated and the cure for that can just be time the next type of lump is lumps caused by inflammation which could be infection or a reaction to the product these are different because they need a Medical Response if you have a lump that grows over time and it's particularly if it's asymmetrical and there's redness around it could be the start of an infection or an actual infection or it could be a reaction which typically involves the rest of the lip also swelling up but they can go very lumpy when the product is reacting with your immune system so those are the core types of lumps that you may face as a clinician or a patient and the next question is how do we actually avoid them in the first place so in terms of avoiding non-inflammatory lesions those are basically just lumps of filler the biggest thing you can do is just make sure that after you've done the procedure you do a gentle check for any lumps or bumps that you can feel on that day because if you leave a large bolus of filler it tends to attract moisture and it tends to become firmer over the next few days whereas if you massage before the patient leaves and I always

of filler it tends to attract moisture
and it tends to become firmer over the
next few days whereas if you massage
before the patient leaves and I always

say we smooth it don't move it so it's a gentle squeeze with that respects the shape of the lip you're trying to create and gently integrates the filler into the lip tissue rather than just squashes it then you should decrease the amount of follow-ups that come from these non-inflammatory lumps in terms of avoiding inflammatory lesions a lot of this is down to injection technique trying to keep the lip area as clean as possible while you're injecting not applying makeup particularly makeup that's not sterile after the proced anything that seals in a bug might be a problem I also think you can decrease the risk a little bit by trying not to make as many entry points as often we end up doing so try and reduce the number of entry points because each entry point is a potential entry point for bacteria it might also be worth doing procedures on days when you're not doing anything else particularly dental procedures the more bacteria enters the bloodstream around the patient's head the more likely some of those bacteria May settle onto an area filler and then you end up with lumps and bumps that are caused by inflammation down to bacteria

which are the hardest type to treat so depending on the type of lump that you are faced with you can either give it more time if you think it's just to do with post-procedural swelling you can massage it if you think it's to do with placement of filler or a large bolus that's distracting for the patient or you can get into treating the medical causes of lumps or bumps which are infection and allergic reaction once you can deal with all of those you should have a sequence on which you can apply intervention sensibly doing the minimal intervention necessary and saw most patients back to a happy state within the first or second visit but you will find the more you think about this topic and the more you improve your injection Technique post-procedure massage that the number of lumps you get should reduce over time although they'll never get to zero it's a pretty common side effect after Lia so assuming you've now done all the light touch interventions with massage and time of course you have got the final Sledgehammer which is you can dissolve all the lumps with hyon days now it's a good idea to try not to do this and until you know that that's your best option so I definitely would
